# 탭 69

* \[Applause\] this game has seen no shortage of balance patches and like any other game after a while some players have started complaining about power creep being able to breathe on dry land used to be an incredibly unique and Powerful perk but now it's basically part for the course with around half of the player base being able to see and respond to the actions of other players used to be an incredible Advantage one that literally invalidated a huge number of popular play Styles the same could be said about warm-blooded respiration pair bonding  
* and Offspring rearing you social and even flight and yet even after an uncountable number of game updates balance patches and expansion packs one of the most consistent builds in The Meta is the jellyfish a collection of ancient builds which usually have a grand total of zero of the aformentioned abilities today we're going to discuss why the jellyfish as well as other narian and jelly-like builds have been able to stand the test of time we'll talk about the most notable members of the faction and place them all on the  
* jellyfish tier list but first an overview of the the history of the jellyfish build plus a quick rundown of the typical stats and abilities they tend to have jellyfish have some of the strangest stat spreads in the entire game the most obvious Quirk is that their intelligence and perception stats are basically zero jellyfish have no central nervous system and so essentially all intelligence and perception-based abilities are completely locked on their skill tree the lack of a brain means jellyfish have to play mostly on autopilot and rely on  
* pre-programmed reflexes and instincts to steer them away from danger and T food but those aren't their only extremely weird stats jellyfish possess some incredible regenerative abilities and can recover from basically any damage which is good because jellyfish have next to no defense at all they're comprised of around 95% water and their outer layer is a simple jelly-like substance that offers very little protection luckily because of the fact they're almost entirely composed of water they blend in quite well with the  
* water many of them are nearly invisible and all are translucent to some extent which makes them EX extremely difficult to detect via eyesight what most players know about jellyfish though is how absolutely cracked their damage stat is the jellyfish's special ability nyst allows for massive Venom damage upon even the slightest contact with their tentacles this isn't Universal as some jellyfish possess far more potent Venom than others a topic will cover extensively during the tier list portion of this video but nonetheless most  
* competent players fear getting too close to any jellyfish because of their deadly reputation their stat spread may seem completely nonsensical Until you realize what the meta was like when the jellyfish was first added to the game I've covered quote unquote ancient builds before on this channel builds like the shark Scorpion and centipede but jellyfish vastly predate all of them jellyfish were one of the first if not the first major faction of multicellular builds and so they had very little need for defenses and intelligence when there  
* was very little to defend yourself from or to outsmart drifting along filter feeding on Plankton worked just fine some might argue it still does even though filopods fish and arthropods were added to the game soon after jellyfish remained extremely popular so after 500 million years of balance patches and game updates let's see which jellyfish builds come out on top at the bottom of the tier list we've got the moon jelly one of the most common jellyfish builds in the game and yet one that doesn't really make use of  
* the jellyfish builds more interesting abilities moon jellies have uniquely short tentacles which deal fairly minimal damage this makes them much less of a threat than basically all other jellies in addition to that moon jellies are notably more nutritious than other jellyfish making them common targets for any player that's able to survive their fairly minimal defenses in fact moon jellyfish are one of the only jellyfish which are actively prayed upon by other jellyfish even when fully grown the moon jelly play style is entirely passive  
* relying on its quick respawn rate and strong match up against Plankton to make it worthwhile moon jellies are becoming a popular choice in the recent meta as they have a natural resistance to microplastic poisoning negating one of the main challenges as other Marine players have had to learn to adapt to recently so it is a solid starter build for players trying to learn the ocean meta but one that I think most competent players will quickly move on from next in F tier we have the comb jelly comb jellies are some of the most  
* unique jellyfish builds in the game in fact even calling them jellyfish is contentious as they are technically not part of the narian faction nonetheless they are gelatinous and have most of the same abilities as jellyfish so I wanted to include them anyway their main difference is that com jellies do not move by pulsing their Bell and instead swim by using rows of cyia this offers a slightly greater degree of agility and control compared to your standard jellyfish build their tentacles are also slightly different being lined with  
* adhesive glue rather than venomous microb barbs this is quite efficient at capturing small prey but is completely useless on defense and because they still do not possess an advanced nervous system the fact that they have greater control over their movement via their cilia doesn't amount to that big of an advantage in practice they still can't Dodge Predator attacks because they cannot Dodge an attack that they don't know is coming this build has a lot of potential if they can just put a few more points into intelligence but for  
* now I'm still going to place them in F tier at the bottom of D tier we have the C nettle the C nettle is essentially the most basic average jellyfish build in the game it embodies every feature of the archetype yet excels at none of them and doesn't really have any additional unique abilities the sea nettle has decent length tentacles that can inflict solid damage potentially lethal to builds within its own weight class but still not strong enough to really Merit the Extreme Caution the reputation has impressed on the player base with low  
* medium size and the same nearly non-existent nervous system sea Nettles may be more well defended than the moon jelly but with even the slightest bit of clever counterplay they still fall apart immediately next in D tier we have the coral you might be surprised to see coral on the jellyfish tier list and indeed I am stretching the definition a little bit but they are a part of the narian faction and are less different once you look more closely their early game is pretty much identical to the jellyfish but rather than remaining free  
* floating they settle onto rocks and surfaces along the seabed and attached to a single point dropping their Mobility stat to zero for the remainder of their playthrough to compensate corals then activate their signature ability calcify constructing an incredibly sturdy exoskeleton around themselves and massively buffing their defense stat in the process as this exoskeleton grows the coral will also clone itself eventually granting the player control over an entire colony of coral Al to accomplish one thing AFK  
* experience farming once established the hard part of their playthrough is over and they can now filter feed for essentially as long as they like so an easy build to play if you can make it past their vulnerable planktonic early game although unfortunately the climate change balance patch seems to be a pretty harsh Nerf to this play \[Music\] style and at the top of D tier we have the other Colonial naderian the Mana War this build Sports medium damage much higher than the moon jelly or sea nettle but but still not the instant death  
* level Venom that some of the higher tier jellyfish have like other jellyfish it has low ability and terrible defenses but I would say that the manow war is uniquely vulnerable to predation due to the fact that rather than having a bell to propel itself through the water instead the manow war has a balloon making them very easy to detect and attack from above where many seabirds hunt in addition any damage to the balloon could easily cause it to deflate sending the man War straight to the depth for an instant game over which  
* negates the advantage most jelly play fish have of being able to regenerate from minor to moderate damage lastly the Mana War seems to be uniquely vulnerable to getting beat during severe storms likely also due to their positioning on the surface of the water where they get beaten around by the waves and the wind still not something you want to be trading hits with if you're a fish in C tier we have the Lion's man this is the largest jellyfish build in the game sporting a massive HP stat and tentacles that can reach length of 120  
* ft longer than the blue whale end to end giant as it may be its power stat is still below average as its venom still isn't really enough to take down anything much larger than a minnow you would think a bigger jellyfish would go after bigger prey but similar to the whale it uses its large size to hit a larger area with its attacks with the hope of getting frequent multi-kills its large size makes it very difficult to kill simply by nature of it being able to literally survive half of itself being eaten and there isn't much out  
* there that would need to eat more than half still with its stings being being fairly weak they aren't really threatening enough to Merit placement in the higher tiers moving on up to B tier we have the anemon similar to Coral anemones are closely related to jellyfish but opt for a sedentary play style rather than a free roaming one unlike Coral though anemones protect themselves using powerful venomous Stingers rather than a hard calcified exoskeleton rather than filter feeding on Plankton like corals do they also use this Venom to catch and  
* kill prey many of them possess extremely powerful Venom enough to cause organ failure even in larger builds like humans now normally being completely immobile is a huge vulnerability however the reason I rank anemon so high in spite of this weakness is that anemones are some of the best team combo builds in the entire game forming excellent team comps with both clown fish and crabs in the case of the clownish the anemon and the clownish cover each other's weaknesses deterring the Predators that the other is vulnerable  
* to similar to jellyfish anemones are vulnerable to sea turtles as they are resistant to their venom clownish valiantly fight for their teammate even against those sea turtles which are gargantuan by comparison and in return predatory fish that would love to score an easy kill on a small vulnerable fish need to tank a mouthful of pneumatics to even have a chance at attacking a clownish and in the crabb's case rather than fighting off Turtles the crab will actually attach the enemity to itself gaining Venom damage on its own attacks  
* in exchange for granting the enemity its Mobility back so while probably only low to mid-tier on its own the combo potential of this build makes it an optimal choice in many scenarios and bumps it up to B tier and finally jumping all the way up to S tier we have the box jelly when it comes to overpowered Venom there is quite literally not a single character in the game that has the Box jelly beat its neurotoxic venom is extremely deadly rapidly paralyzing the victim quickly leading to Suffocation and cardiac  
* arrest in addition to having the highest damage of any jellyfish Fox jellies also have the highest stealth stat while many jellyfish sport a wide range of translucent I colors like red and blue fox jellyfish are almost completely transparent as close to Invisible as possible in addition because they sport fewer tentacles and therefore experience less hydrodynamic drag and because of their unique Bell shape which allows them to Jet through the water box jellyfish have the highest Mobility stat of any jelly this makes them incredibly  
* difficult to detect and avoid as they Zoom through Coastal Waters leading many players to Simply avoid the territory entirely rather than risk potential encounters the Hallmark of a high tier build and as if all of that wasn't cracked enough box jellyfish are unique in that they're the only jellyfish which Have Eyes allowing them to detect danger and avoid obstacles this enables them to thrive in less open areas where prey like to hide such as kelp forests or mangrove swamps coupled with their higher Mobility stat this actually  
* enables the box jellyfish to actively pursue prey rather than simply drifting with the current and hoping to catch something with the exception of tankiness and tentacle attack range box jellyfish are strictly Superior to all other jellies which is why there's such an outlier on the tier list I also wanted to highlight a unique member of the Box jelly faction the ironi the uroni is a dwarf variant of the Box jelly maxing out at only an inch or twoo long yet also Sports the same incredibly potent Toxin and all the other special  
* traits the Box jelly possesses plus one additional trait Bell Stingers most jellyfish have a pretty massive weak point their Bell it can be attacked safely without the risk of being stung so long as the attacker avoids the tentacles the urangi simply does not have this weakness as every millimeter of its surface is covered in the same deadly Stingers as a result even accidentally bumping into the aoni can result in an instant game over which has a lot of players worried given how much more popular this build has become  
* lately this is obviously a super risky play style though as the urangi itself has the lowest HP stat of any build I've ever covered on this channel even just swimming into an obstacle is damaging enough to kill an irangi so they need to play extremely carefully and can't take advantage of the obstacle heavy environments that the other box jellies can so that's the jellyfish tier list generally I rate most of them low to mid-tier with the exception of the truly formidable outlier of the Box jelly before we wrap up I do want to cover  
* some counterplay options I mentioned it a few times in the video but sea turtles have a Flatout un losable match up against jellyfish being completely immune to their venom and so they are able to safely attack and digest them but another lesser known but even more effective counter to jellyfish f is the sea slug not only are they immune to the Venom but they can actually absorb the stingers and convert them into their own defensive weapons basically giving them all the deadly benefits of the jellyfish without the drawback of being basically  
* brainless I expect both of these builds to become a lot more popular as jellyfish numbers continue to increase so if you're looking to score some easy wins I recommend learning the strategies of both of these excellent builds I hope to do a se- slug videos soon so be on the lookout for that and be sure to subscribe so you don't miss it when it drops and speaking of not missing important video releases I'd like to remind all my viewers that the best way to watch my content is with nebula the sponsor of this video it's best for you  
* because you'll get access to all my videos as well as videos from hundreds of other excellent creators early and adree and it's best for me because watching on nebula supports my channel better than anywhere else helping me bring you the highest quality original content that I can these 3D animations in my recent videos aren't cheap to create but I know you guys like them and I want to make sure this channel looks the absolute best that it can now while you could just pay sticker price as one of the owners of nebula I know a couple  
* of tricks for how to get it even cheaper including one we haven't talked about in ads before there are two main ways first you may remember a while back that I promoted a bundle Subscription Service containing both curiosity stream and nebula many of you sign up for that in order to support me and I appreciate that but unfortunately that Arrangement has ended luckily nebula never updated their pricing so you can still get nebula for the same crazy low price of only 250 a month if you go with their annual subscription the other way is to

# 탭 70

* \[Music\] \[Applause\] this video was made in partnership with curiositystream reptiles are a diverse faction full of many strategies and generally speaking they tend to be fairly straightforward turtles are tank builds that spec into high defensive stats at the cost of mobility crocodiles are damage dealers that focus on grappling to secure kills snakes optimize for stealth and rely on status effects to bring down their targets but where do lizards fit into the meta they aren't really known for extreme speed but as far as cold-blooded builds go  
* they're quicker than most and while not as tanky as turtles and not as stealthy as snakes they can hold their own in each of these departments as well some of the larger lizards can even give crocodilians a run for their money in the power category too while many of them could best be described as jack of all trades generalists there are also plenty of lizards with extremely unique abilities so to better understand the many different game plans employed by the lizard player base today we're going through the lizard  
* tier list before we get into the tier list though first i'd like to start with some background on the history of blizzard faction so back during the carboniferous expansion the meta revolved around the conflict between the two most powerful factions the amphibians and the arthropods however during this time a small subsection of the amphibian player base unlocked two new special abilities the eggshell which allowed them to respawn on dry land rather than in the water and scales which gave them massively increased dehydration resistance  
* this eliminated the reliance on water and allowed them to explore tons of new strategies eventually diversifying into all of the reptile builds in today's meta as well as a few that were so overpowered that they ended up getting nerfed the most powerful lizard build of all time was the mosasaurus a build that arose from player base of beach dwelling monitor lizards that specked into aquatic mobility options in order to avoid being griefed by dinosaurs but one of the most powerful lizards of today's meta we're actually going to begin indeed  
* here as i don't think there are any truly awful lizard builds the adaptations they've made to eliminate the weaknesses of their amphibian ancestors is enough to escape bottom tier in my opinion so how do lizards stack up let's get into the tier list in d tier we have the skink a smaller lizard build with very little in the way of unique special abilities their stats are also quite low and their game plan generally just consists of chasing down players in the lower weight classes and struggles pretty hard when forced to  
* defend itself in particular this build has a lot of trouble dealing with birds despite the fact that it often hunts in grassy fields it lacks the stealth stats required to avoid detection from above in fact many skinks are known for being brightly colored or having one specifically brightly colored body part skinks try to avoid being griefed by digging burrows for themselves and while burrows are a great hiding place the burrowing playstyle tends to work best when you've got the ability to launch a powerful attack in a single  
* direction since the bottleneck of a burrow makes it near impossible for a predator to approach without getting hit skinks can't really mount any sort of attack so if their burrow gets discovered it's most likely game over the one defensive ability they do have is one commonly found in the lizard player base autotomy autonomy allows the user to drop their tail in order to get one free escape from a combo or grab now to be fair being able to break out of your opponent's death combos is an extremely useful trait that can  
* save you from certain defeat but with that said autonomy is extremely costly to use isn't guaranteed to work and leaves you with reduced max hp for the rest of the game even if your character has regenerative abilities for the larger lizard builds losing your tail also removes one of your best offensive options but for the skink this isn't as big a problem with all that being said skinks are one of the more lackluster lizard builds so let's keep going and get to the more interesting ones also in detail we have the basilisk  
* lizard which is most notable for its use of the sprint ability not just to move fast on land but also to cross bodies of water at first this ability seems quite impressive and it certainly is a flashy move but upon further examination i don't think it's really all that useful in the long run being on the surface of the water is quite possibly the most vulnerable position in the game leaving you open to attacks from above and below but ultimately i think it's more important to just point out that the whole reason the reptile faction  
* split off an amphibian player base in the first place was to remove their reliance on water so choosing an ability with a pretty situational use that also requires that the user be on the water's edge to use it is kind of a step backwards in the grand scheme of things the basilisk doesn't even have bad stats it couldn't use the water dash move without above average mobility but still i think its choice of moveset is pretty self-limiting and they'd be better off specking into other signature moves in seed here we have two lizard builds  
* who invested heavily into the climbing ability the gecko build operates in a similar manner to the tree frog with cling pads enabling it to climb any surface with ease as far as mobility perks though this is probably one of the best in the game it works great as an escape option but is also fantastic for utility and even for offense allowing the user to approach targets that are normally harder to reach being able to hang on tight is also super important for arboreal combat as fall damage is a major threat with  
* such a strong clinging ability geckos can often be the ones tossing other players into the void below geckos do have a fair number of weaknesses though they opt to use their tails to store energy which can be a useful ability but unfortunately in doing so they take away the option to use their tails to attack geckos also do not have scales instead opting for skin that gives disease resistance bonuses so their armor class is lower than that of most lizards and actually more akin to amphibians because of this they're quite vulnerable  
* to piercing damage such as bird talons and snake fangs and with relatively low damage output their only real hope against such attacks is stealth which some do definitely excel at all in all solid mid tier the enoli is a small agile lizard build with a surprising number of special abilities most notable of which are their adhesive toe pads and their color changing skin most builds that run the cling pad ability tend to be more bulky and cumbersome anoles emphatically break this mold being both excellent climbers and having  
* a climbing speed that rivals that of the squirrel because of this they're great at escaping attacks by jumping between trees and scrambling up branches but where things really get interesting is with the color change perk now the ability to change colors is one of the most downright broken stealth perks in the game and one of the main reasons i consider cephalopods top tier annulis have probably the most basic version of the color change perk allowing them to shift between green and brown at will while nowhere near as versatile as some  
* other color changing builds green and brown are pretty much all you'd need the vast majority of the time this change is very gradual though so it's unlikely to allow you to escape mid combat which is unfortunate because similar to the gecko the annoli suffers from a lack of combat options when caught out in the open last in seats here we have the gila monster the gila monster is one of the tankier lizard builds and possesses one of the highest damage outputs in the game for its size it owes this high damage not to its  
* actual power staff but to its venomous bite a rare ability among lizards this makes them extremely dangerous in close combat and combined with their excellent defensive stats and digging ability this allows them to easily vanquish all the players in any nest it discovers the gila monster is extremely slow moving though which can cause issues if it's ever forced to flee or if it needs to chase down a target the lack of a long whip-like tail also means its defensive combat options aren't near as diverse as other larger  
* lizards still with one of the most devastating bikes in the game it's certainly no low tier in b tier we've got the tried and true iguana arguably the best representative of the lizard build as a whole as it has essentially all the main weaknesses and strengths of its class i have an entire video on the iguana so go check that out if you want an in-depth review but in short the iguana sports mid-range stats across the board and is the first lizard on the tailors to make use of its tail for something other than a sacrificial  
* scapegoat the iguana's tail whip attack allows it to control a solid defensive zone around itself in all directions enabling it to defend itself from group attacks tail whips damage is on the low side though so a persistent attacker could potentially just eat the damage in order to break through and land its own attacks the iguana also has access to the autonomy ability but this is especially costly in the iguanas case as it'd also be giving up its best combat move in the process overall a solid mid tier with lots of potential  
* especially in the marine servers \[Music\] the chameleon build sports several of the most unique abilities in the game so most lizards especially the insectivorous ones have a pretty simple game plan for taking down enemies chase them down and grab them this strategy has a lot of limitations though first of all it means you've got pretty much no options to attack a player who is too large to grab it also forces you to optimize heavily towards mobility forcing you to neglect other potential strategies in order to have a chance at  
* catching up to your target the chameleon subverts this limitation by speccing into one of the strangest abilities in the game a tethered tongue attack this essentially functions as a ranged grab and is extremely effective at scoring chameleon kills this also allows the chameleon the freedom to prioritize other stats over mobility and in the chameleon's case they're clearly optimized for stealth as evidenced by their other special ability color change now while chameleons can change color in order to buff their camouflage  
* most use it for communication and intimidation shifting to brighter colors to display dominance while they don't have the same cling pads that geckos and anoles do their prehensile tail gives them extra security to ensure that they don't get easily ripped out of trees and allows them to attack from riskier positions with all this said though they still struggle when caught out in the open because of their low mobility and they also share the same offensive weakness of not being able to take down players that they can't gulp down in a  
* single bite the tegu is a lizard build that has long been overlooked but has recently become a serious contender on the tier list its recent invasion into the north american server has destabilized the local meta and when you look at their stats it's easy to see why tegus are similar in both strategy and stats to the iguana but slightly slower and more defense oriented their thicker hide and stockier build allows them to shake off attacks that would send other characters reeling they've also got a higher intelligence  
* stat than most lizards which in addition to making them surprisingly decent support builds also makes them great at outsmarting builds like alligators in order to attack their nests tegus are also omnivores meaning they aren't forced to participate in pvp in order to level up they did spec into the autonomy ability though which i unfortunately think keeps them out of top tier before i reveal the top tier lizards i just want to say real quick if you've made it this far and are enjoying the video it would sure make my day if you  
* subscribed thanks so without a shadow of a doubt the best loser build is the monitor lizard they have everything they've got the best stats a combination of great mobility power size and intelligence similar to the iguana monitor lizards have an extremely powerful tail whip capable of deterring even top tier predators monitor lizards also have a venomous bite which again is a rarity in the lizard faction while not the speediest lizards outright they do have great versatility in their movement being able to swim efficiently  
* as well as climb most surfaces though not to the same degree as the marine iguana or the gecko respectively their high intelligence enables them to consistently figure out ways to successfully raid the spawn points of other players and their high defense makes it so they can be rather difficult to stop once they've committed to an attack this faction also contains the most powerful lizard in the game a lizard that reaches near dinosaur power levels komodo dragons are extremely powerful they have a venomous bite that while not  
* as powerful as cobra venom is still powerful enough to seriously weaken even the tankiest builds but even without that they'd be an absolute powerhouse their tail swipes claw slashes and thrashing bites can easily shred through the hp of their targets their only real weakness is their speed which while solid isn't broken by any means still with their incredible sense of smell a player can never really truly escape a komodo that's hunting them the distance that you need to flee in order to make the komodo unable to track  
* you down would require you to leave the island server entirely last but certainly not least komodos as well as the entire monitor lizard player base understand that their tails are an invaluable weapon that shouldn't be sacrificed in order to escape a battle so there you have it the lizard tier list hopefully now you've got a good understanding of the lizard strategies and match-ups admittedly there were a few things i had to cut from the script of this video to avoid demonetization or age restriction if you want to see the director's cut of  
* this episode you can check it out on nebula a streaming service made by and for creators i do this with all of my new videos uploading ad-free and uncensored versions of my videos there as well as a few entirely exclusive videos in addition you can check out my appearance on the nebula exclusive podcast genesis if you're interested in hearing my character's backstory nebula has partnered with curiositystream to bring you both services for less than 15 bucks a year which is 26 off the price of what curiosity stream alone normally goes for  
* curiosity stream if you somehow didn't already know is another amazing streaming service where you can watch thousands of high quality documentaries including amazing dino world a documentary about the aforementioned mosasaurus to get access head to curiositystream.com tirsu thanks again to curiositystream for partnering with us at nebula thank you to my patrons on patreon and thank you for watching \[Music\] you

# 탭 71

* \[Music\] \[Music\] Mollisks are one of the most diverse factions in the entire game. Indeed, much more diverse than I think most players realize. They're extremely popular both among casual and competitive players. With some builds demanding incredible levels of skill to pilot, while others are practically AFK, and with so many different potential Mollisk builds to choose from, it can be difficult to know which builds are actually competitively viable in the current meta.  
* So, today we're going to go through the Mollisk tier list. While I attempt to cover as many of the popular well-known builds as I can, Mollisks are an extremely broad category, second only to arthropods in terms of number of individual members. So, inevitably, some are getting left out of this video for the sake of time. Still, by highlighting standout builds in both the high and low tiers, hopefully I can give you a good understanding of where the Mollisk faction fits into the current metag game and will be able to fill in the gaps for  
* yourself. But before we dive into the tier list, what is a mollisk and what are the common attributes? Mollisks are a huge group encompassing all gastropods, all by valves, and all seephopods. While every group has its outliers, most factions at least have some consistency in their base stats. Birds tend to have pretty high mobility.  
* Mammals tend to have high intelligence. Artropods high defense, and so on. With mollisks, though, it's pretty hard to pin down any obvious through line. Pick any one stat, and you can probably find a mollisk both at the top and bottom of the charts. But one thing you will not find is a mollisk with basic middle-of the road stats.  
* While the numbers and combinations will vary greatly between builds, one thing I've noticed is that just about every mollusk has something extreme about it with at least one stat being either incredibly high or abysmally low. But anyway, given just how hard it is to generalize them, we may as well just dive straight into the tier list.  
* At the bottom of F tier, we have the common slug. Now, while this build may look like it has all the downsides of the snail without any of the benefits of the snail's protective shell, yeah, it's pretty much exactly what it looks like. The Garden Slug genuinely has some of the worst stats in the entire game, with even its best stat being fairly mid.  
* Its only defensive ability is its slime, which does help ward off attacks from insects. It's a double-edged sword, though, as their slime also leaves a trail, making slugs one of the easiest builds in the game to track down. Now, I will admit there are some advantages to not having a shell with the main one being that slugs can slip through much smaller cracks and hide underground much more easily.  
* However, since they need to come out to feed anyway, this doesn't really help them avoid predation all that well. So, with all these negatives and pretty much nothing else going for them, the snail without a shell is going in F tier. Next in F tier, we have the common clam. A build that exemplifies the extreme stat lines I was talking about to a tea.  
* With no eyes, terrible movement abilities, and no attacking moves, and no brain, the clam has to rely on only two stats, both of which are quite high. The first is their stealth on account of the clam's surprisingly decent burrowing ability. By wedging themselves into the seabed, they can stay out of sight from most potential threats.  
* But if an enemy manages to find them in spite of this, the clam has some of the heaviest armor of any mollisk, being able to deflect some pretty substantial damage. But with no ability to counterattack or escape, eventually a persistent attacker will deal enough damage to spell game over. Well, okay, technically clams can move.  
* But I mean, come on. In the current meta, this just isn't enough. What's worse is that even though clams can burrow down to hide, in order to survive, they need to filter feed. And to do that, they must poke their most vulnerable weak point above the surface of the seabed, giving away their hiding spot and opening themselves up to a devastating critical hit.  
* At the top of F tier, we have the common snail. The garden snail has most of the same weaknesses as the previous two entries on the tier list, but slightly less severe. Nails are every bit as slow and non-threatening as slugs, but they do at least have a shell to help block attacks. They can even use their shell to shield bash attackers in a pinch, though this doesn't do much damage.  
* However, similar to the clam because of how slow their movement speed is and how weak their counterattacks are, eventually an aggressive player will be able to punch through their defenses. So, they're not the worst of the worst. But compared to some of the unique and powerful abilities we'll see higher up on the tier list, the basic snail build just isn't going to cut it.  
* At the bottom of Dtier, we have the scallop. The Scallop is essentially a more decked out version of the Clam, though it has a lower base defense stat than the Clam, its weaknesses are also much less pronounced. Instead of being completely immobile, scallops, oddly enough, have the jet propulsion ability, something usually only found in sephalopods and box jellies.  
* Though it has a high cool down, this ability gives them an escape option if they're ever under direct attack. Something every other build on this list so far desperately needs. Of course, being able to flee from battle wouldn't do them much good if they couldn't see attacks coming. But thankfully, scallops also spec into primitive eyes, enabling them to detect predators and reposition themselves accordingly, in theory.  
* Anyway, one of the issues with simple eyes is that even the most basic stealth techniques, such as, for example, moving very slowly, can get past their danger sense, resulting in scallops getting regularly bodied by starfish. So, while they're definitely a bit more versatile than some mollisks, I can't give them too much credit.  
* Next in Dtier, we're going to have the first of the sephopods on our tier list, the chambered Nautilus. The Nautilus is a slowmoving armored scavenger build that sustains itself mostly on detritus and carryon that is sunk to the bottom of the sea. While being able to swim while rocking a tough shell is impressive, their terrible swim speed shows just how power crept this ancient build has gotten.  
* Their perception stat is also pretty mid, especially for a sephopod, which on average have some of the best vision of any marine build. The Nautilus used to be a much more popular build until seals and sea lions were added to the game, as their high agility and armor-piercing bite gave them a crushing matchup versus the Nautilus.  
* So, while it is impressive that the Nautilus has made it through some of the roughest balanced patches in the game's history, the fact that they were driven to the depths by seals doesn't scream high tier to me. First in C tier, we have the Geographic Cone. This marine snail build rocks pretty much all the same strengths and weaknesses as your average snail with one major exception.  
* This snail can one-shot just about anything. The geographic cone has a venomous harpoon which it uses to hunt fish, but it can also use it to defend itself in a pinch. Now, the geographic cone is still incredibly slow and really not all that stealthy, so actually landing a hit on a fish is a bit of a challenge. Still, this is one gastropod you really don't want to underestimate.  
* The cone snail's harpoon pierces armor, so any starfish man who thinks that all mollisks are easy pickings is in for a rude awakening. Next in Ctier, we have the Dumbo octopus. This is one of the strangest sephopod builds in the entire game and lacks a lot of the signature abilities players normally associate with the octopus, but it also has a mix of other unique traits that normal octopuses don't.  
* You see, the Dumbo octopus is a deep sea build, and on that server, the normal stealth techniques simply don't apply. color changing and ink clouds aren't going to do a player any good when most of the major threats aren't relying on vision anyway. So even though those abilities are what the octopus is best known for, unfortunately the Dumbo octopus has to find other ways to remain hidden from predators.  
* And unfortunately this means it needed to make even more trade-offs. Rather than using jet propulsion, which is a conspicuous and turbulent but incredibly effective movement ability, the Dumbo octopus swims using large fins on the side of his mantle. This is significantly slower, but a great deal stealthier, too.  
* This makes it too slow to catch fish, but it's still able to thrive off of its winning matchup versus crustaceans. Its webbed tentacles are perfect for surrounding and capturing targets on the seafloor. So, definitely an interesting build, but by having to sacrifice so much of what makes the octopus so overpowered, the Dumbo octopus is definitely one of the lower tier ones.  
* And at the top of Ctier, we also have the first squid on our list, the bobtail squid. This is another sephalopod which uses a fairly unorthodox ability to boost its stealth. As one of the smallest sephopods in the game, the bobtail squid really cannot take any hits. So, in order to avoid most of the ocean's major threats, the bobtail buries itself during the day and only comes out at night.  
* However, even under cover of darkness, the bobtail squid would still be at risk of being attacked. Though it's somewhat counterintuitive, on a night where the moon and stars are unobscured, detecting another player in the water isn't as hard as you might think. The shadows they cast in the water are highly visible and give away a player's location immediately, making it very easy for predators lurking below to strike.  
* The bobtail squid deals with this by specking into an extremely rare and strange ability, bioluminescence. It may seem backwards to hide by glowing, but doing so obscures the squid by removing its shadow. Of course, this does make them easier to detect from above, but attacks at night from seabirds are much less common. The Bobtail may have the lowest stats of any seephopod, but its unique and creative way of hiding in plain sight keeps it out of the low tiers.  
* I can't give it too much credit, though, because while its strategy for stealth at night is clever, honestly, it's not that good at hiding during the daytime. Having a seemingly worse ability to camouflage itself due to its bright coloration. At the bottom of B tier, we have the leaf sheep, a sea slug with a truly unique ability called kleptolasty.  
* Leaf sheep is able to absorb the chloroplasts from the algae it consumes, essentially stealing the algae's photosynthesis ability. This ability generates XP passively over time, allowing them to grow, heal, and regenerate their energy without eating. It's hard to overstate how game-breaking this ability would be if it weren't on a character with otherwise pathetic base stats.  
* For a human, this would basically be like if sitting in the sun provided you with the same benefits as eating well and working out. Unlike some of the sea slugs higher up on this list, the leaf sheep doesn't really have any defensive abilities. But if you want a character that is extremely forgiving in terms of the resource management side of things, the leaf sheep may actually be the best build in the game for anyone looking for that chill, casual play style.  
* On pretty much the exact opposite end of the spectrum, next in B tier, we have the giant squid. Now, obviously, this thing's HP stat is extreme with it being the largest mollisk in the game. But one thing I bet you weren't expecting was for it to also have the highest perception stat of any mollisk. I mentioned before that in the dark depths of the ocean, the normal stealth mechanics don't apply.  
* Well, the giant squid is the exception to this. Its eyes are so large, the largest in the game in fact, that even with only a tiny fraction of a percent of sunlight making it to the depths, the giant squid can still see. This is bad news for all the deep sea builds that assumed camouflage wasn't necessary and specked their character accordingly.  
* This incredible eyesight is necessary for more than just hunting, though. Being gigantic, unfortunately, makes you extremely easy to detect via echolocation, so they have to remain vigilant constantly to avoid being ambushed by the abyssal server's true apex predator. Luckily, their vision has a range just barely more than that of the whale's sonar.  
* So, as long as they're on top of their game, they should be able to outmaneuver and avoid attacks. On this same tier, I'm also going to include the colossal squid, which has many of the same traits as the giant squid, including massive eyes and incredible dark vision. The colossal squid does have a few subtle differences in its kit, though.  
* It's slightly heavier, but also less mobile, relying on ambushes, using its two extra long arms to capture prey, as opposed to the giant squid's more traditional method of rushing down a target with an array of tentacles. The colossal squid also has hooks on its arms which do more damage and can inflict bleed buildup, making them much more dangerous for whales to attack, making up for their slower swim speed.  
* However, at the end of the day, both the giant squid and colossal squid have terrible matchups against whales. And because of their large size, their energy upkeep costs are insane. So, they have to play pretty much a perfect game to avoid getting smoked. Both are powerful builds if you know what you're doing, but not without their limitations.  
* At the bottom of A tier, we have another sea slug, the blue sea dragon. Similar to the leaf sheep, this slug also has the power to copy the ability of its main food source. But in this case, the blue dragon isn't consuming algae. It's consuming jellyfish. The blue sea dragon is able to absorb the venomous pneumaticists from the tentacles of their prey, and in doing so gains a massive buff in the form of an equally deadly sting at the tip of its frills.  
* This is incredible as the blue sea dragon is able to gain the most powerful ability of the jellyfish faction without any of the awful downsides like you know being literally brainless and having no control over their own movement. Blue sea dragons also have much better stealth than a lot of sea slugs using the counter shading technique with a bright underbelly and dark blue backside making them hard to see both from above and below.  
* While not quite as OP as some of the sephalopod mollisks, the blue sea dragon definitely stands above all other slugs and snails as a unique and formidable outlier. Next in A tier, we have the common octopus. A solid representative of the octopus faction as a whole. It possesses the core abilities that define the octopus build along with its inherent vulnerabilities.  
* Its two standout strengths are stealth and intelligence. The common octopus boasts some of the best stealth in the game. Capable of changing both its color and texture to blend seamlessly into virtually any environment. Dense weeds, jagged rocks, or barren seabeds. And if its camouflage fails and it's ever spotted, it can deploy an ink cloud to temporarily debuff the attacker's perception stat, usually buying enough time to escape.  
* However, escape is often its only viable option, as the octopus has some of the lowest defenses in the game. With no armor whatsoever, any hit it takes can deal devastating damage. Even in a server as perilous as the ocean, where threats abound regardless of armor, the common octopus must play cautiously. Bites from everyday fish can pose a serious risk.  
* So despite its impressive abilities, it requires careful strategy to survive. Speaking of bites, though, one lesserk known but important ability the octopus has is venom, which it delivers via a bite with its sharp beak. Finally, now is as good a time as any to say that although it's not a combat weakness, the octopus's biggest weakness is its low lifespan.  
* Given that a lot of the octopus's best tricks need to be learned, either by observation or trial and error, the octopus's already powerful intelligence would be a lot more powerful if the devs allowed it to be refined over the course of a few decades, like dolphins, parrots, and humans get. This weakness is pervasive through the entire seephopod faction, which is interesting because some of the other mollisks are pretty much the exact opposite.  
* But yeah, this is why despite their many overpowered abilities, it's hard to give any sephopods a top tier rank unless they're truly broken in even more ways than usual. Ranking slightly higher in A tier, the giant octopus adapts on the octopus archetype with one minor tweak, trading some stealth for greater strength.  
* While it retains the ability to change color and texture to blend into its surroundings and can deploy an ink cloud to escape threats, it's less adept at stealth than the common octopus due to its larger size, which makes it more noticeable when moving. However, this trade-off comes with significant advantages.  
* Its above average bulk gives it a wider margin of error in confrontations. Unlike the typically less brrawy octopus builds, the giant octopus boasts impressive physical power with arms capable of wrestling midsized sharks and a beak that deals substantial slicing damage. In addition to being venomous, it also shares the same high intelligence that other octopuses do, enabling it to tackle problems and improvised solutions on the spot.  
* Though it shares the same lack of armor as other octopuses, leaving it exposed to heavy damage, the giant octopus's enhanced strength and unique abilities more than offset its reduced stealth, marking it as a force to be reckoned with. Next in a tier, we have the Humbult Squid, also known as the Red Devil.  
* The Humbult Squid is the wolf of the ocean. It doesn't have the best stats in any one category. It's not the fastest, it's not the largest, and it's not the deadliest. However, it is well above average in all of those categories, and that plus some impressive hunting tactics makes it one of the most fearsome mollisks in the game.  
* During the day, Humbult squid mains hang out in the deep sea out of sight. But as it gets dark, the hunt begins, and the squid rise from the depths to attack. They hunt in packs, coordinating with each other via pulsing red and white light. These feeding frenzies can tear through schools of fish in an instant and then return back to the depth before builds like the dolphin can arrive to challenge them.  
* Now, obviously, communicating with light pulses has the disadvantage of giving away your location. So, faster characters will usually be able to avoid getting caught in the frenzy, but still the fear factor alone of getting caught surrounded by large glowing red squid has to earn it a spot in A tier.  
* And last in A tier, we have the mimic octopus. The mimic octopus is a smaller mollisk with below average stats for its archetype. However, the mimic octopus uses his color change ability in a truly unique way to shapeshift into the form of other marine characters. In doing so, it can trick other players into misplaying in many different ways.  
* The most obvious strategy is just to mimic a character with a powerful venomous ability like a lion fish or sea snake. This will cause predators to leave you alone for fear of getting stung or bitten. But this technique can also be used on offense to trick a target into a false sense of security by mimicking something less dangerous like a starfish or flatfish.  
* Now, while this is all extremely impressive, it's worth noting that the mimic octopus's options are fairly limited. It's most effective at mimicking characters that already share its black and white stripe appearance. When it attempts to mimic something else, it's admittedly a lot less convincing, and if their disguise fails, their below average base stats leave them in a pretty vulnerable position should an enemy attack.  
* a high-skll, highreward technique, and one that deserves some pretty high recognition, but one that's also easy to overestimate. It's clever, but not quite broken. Okay, we've finally reached the S tier. If you've watched up until this point and are enjoying the video, I'd really appreciate it if you took a second to subscribe to the channel.  
* Thanks. At the bottom of S tier, we have another small mollisk with blue accents, the blue ringed octopus. This is one of the smallest octopus builds in the game, but you wouldn't know it if all you saw was its power staff. The blue ringed octopus has the deadliest venom of any mollisk, even deadlier than the geographic cone.  
* Its venom is the same neurotoxin found in puffer fish, which can one-shot almost anything within only a few minutes. Because of the blue ringed octopus's insanely high damage, this build opts for the aposmatism technique, where rather than attempting to hide from predators using camouflage, when threatened, it instead boldly asserts its presence with vibrant neon blue markings all along its body.  
* In doing so, it is essentially flexing on other Predator players, saying it has no reason to fear them and daring them to make a move. This strat is common among players with powerful toxic abilities, and most players quickly learn that bright colors mean danger. Now, one of the usual drawbacks of this ability is that it dramatically cuts the player's own stealth stat.  
* While this trade-off makes sense when it comes to scaring off predators, if you're a predator yourself, this makes the task of catching prey harder, as they'll be able to see you coming more easily. But because of the octopus's innate color change ability, the blue ringed octopus is able to switch its intimidation ability on and off, allowing it to remain stealthy when it needs to.  
* Now, of course, because it's on the small side, there's always a chance an opponent could overwhelm the octopus and take it down before it had the chance to counterattack. But more often than not, this small octopus is turning the tide on anything that dares challenge it. While it's definitely a glass cannon, the blue- ringed octopus's incredible stats and unique ability to subvert one of its major weaknesses earns it a spot in S tier.  
* And on the topic of subverting weaknesses, next in S tier, we have the coconut octopus. This is the octopus build with the highest intelligence stat of any mollisk, and the only one that makes tool use a core part of its strategy. The octopus's biggest weakness is its pathetic defense stat, and this was actually a direct nerf to overpowered seephalopod builds of the past.  
* Many sephopods used to have thick, sturdy shells like other mollisks do way back during the Ortivision expansion by using their incredibly high intelligence and tool use ability. The coconut octopus is able to regain access to heavy armor by equipping the shells of defeated scallops and clams. This makes them much less vulnerable to being picked off by a random attack from a fish or bird.  
* In addition, kind of similar to the mimic octopus, most players don't have their guard up around a clam, so the shells also provide excellent cover to ambush opponents from. And after scoring a win, they can retreat to the safety of their shell to avoid getting it stolen. Similar to leopards dragging their food up into trees to avoid hyenas.  
* But shells aren't the only tools the coconut octopus has learned to incorporate into its combat repertoire. Using its water siphon, it can also launch debris at fish, making them one of the only builds in the game with access to a ranged attack. This is excellent at dealing chip damage without leaving the safety of the shell, meaning they don't fall into the same trap as clams by relying on defense with no means of counterattacking.  
* Overall, this is truly one of the most impressive showcases of tool use in the entire game other than humans, as it legitimately subverts one of the weaknesses of the typical octopus build without sacrificing any of the perks, an easy S tier rank. And now, our final S tier, the Cuttlefish. The Cuttlefish has a number of impressive stats, including quite literally the highest stealth stat in the game.  
* It has the most advanced version of the color change ability and is able to swap between colors at such an incredible rate that it can basically animate its skin. This means that in addition to standard camouflage, the cuttlefish can also use its appearance to confuse and hypnotize its prey or startle and intimidate its predators. Though it may not have the ability to fire projectiles like the coconut octopus, its grappling arms give it a deceptively long reach, often catching players off guard and setting them up to get comboed into the cuttlefish's  
* venomous bite. Even though the cuttlefish has no abilities which buff its defense, actually landing a hit on this agile and mesmerizing character is genuinely one of the most difficult things in the game, their speed stat is a lot closer to that of a squid than an octopus. Even though the rest of their abilities all align much more closely to the octopus, the cuttlefish combines the most powerful elements from both the squid, like high maneuverability and long reach, and the octopus, like intelligence, venom, and color changing,  
* into one overpowered build. And for that, I'm ranking it as the number one mollisk in the game. And that's the tier list. Overall, the viability of mollisks is super inconsistent. Best sephopods are truly busted, but the faction is also filled with a lot of sub-optimal jank and AFK farming builds.  
* So, there really is something for everyone here, from metat tryards to low tier heroes. Now, knowing which traits and abilities to invest in can be a difficult choice. Luckily for you, I've partnered with the game Cell to Singularity to provide you the perfect training simulation. Cell to Singularity is a scientifically accurate non-fiction idle clicker game where you begin as a single cell and have to unlock traits to evolve your biology, increase your intellect, and invent new technology in order to conquer the universe and reach the ultimate  
* singularity point. It is a relentlessly fun idol game that had me hooked for weeks. I love the charming lowpoly 3D animals that you unlock as you progress through the tree of life. They're currently running a limited time game event focused on the dark depths of the ocean. See if you can spot stuff you learned from this video on the unlock tree.  
* It's free to play and available on Steam, iOS, and Android. If you complete the Deep Sea event and stick around for the next update, you'll get to play through the cat event, an update we actually collaborated on together. If you complete that, you'll even unlock a special Tzu themed badge, which will permanently give your simulation a valuable speed buff.  
* So, please do check this one out. Cell to Singularity is one of my absolute favorite companies to work with and I love their unique way of getting gamers interested in learning about ecology. Again, it is completely free to play. So, click or tap the link in the description or scan the QR code on screen now.  
* Thanks again for watching and as always, good luck out there. \[Music\]

# 탭 73

* \[Music\] All right, everyone. Let's talk sephilopods. So, since they're a really unique build, some of you might not know how to actually get to them on the character select screen. Almost all of the builds we've discussed so far have been vertebrates. But to get to this build, you'll actually need to select the invertebrate section and then find the Mollisk guild.  
* From there, you can join one of three clans. The B valves, the gastropods, and the sephalopods. You're going to go ahead and choose sephalopod because they're by far the strongest build in the guild. Now, here's where you get to make a decision. How many arms do you want? You might think this is an arbitrary decision, but what you choose here actually determines a lot of the class specific skills you have available, as well as some of your base stats.  
* So, for this video, I want to outline how important this decision is by examining the two available classes, their most optimized builds, and their most unique abilities. So, anyways, when building a sephalopod, you're offered a choice, 10 arms or only eight. Let's say you choose 10\. Choosing 10 unlocks the squid subclass.  
* The squid is the premier ocean intelligence, power, and mobility hybrid. It's got reasonably high intelligence and more importantly, extraordinary power because of its armor-piercing beak and one of the top grabs available in the ocean servers. Its grab has the added bonus of inflicting damage when the grab target struggles in attempt to break free.  
* Squids back up this powerful offensive kit with high mobility, which means if a squid player wants to pick a fight, escape is not an option. I mentioned intelligence briefly, but I think it's worth discussing further. The squid's intelligence is exemplified by one of its attributes, which is only available to high intelligence builds, the ability to hunt in packs.  
* Pack hunting makes squids a genuinely oppressive force in the territory that they control. It's worth pointing out that I'm talking about the best of many squid builds, the humbult squid. But that's not to say that this is the only play style available to squid players. You can opt for a tank build by selecting either the giant or colossal squid.  
* This build also allows you to enter the deepest ocean servers, areas of the game very few players ever get to see. If you prefer stealth-based builds, you can also try out the bobtail squid, which is one of the only builds which uses light to hide. Because it emits light, the squid avoids casting a shadow against starlight or moonlight, which counterintuitively makes it one of the most stealthy Nocturnal builds.  
* Fun fact, the squid can't activate this stealth ability on its own. It's actually a buff applied by a support class from Kingdom Bacteria called Vibrio Fishery. But I'm getting off track. Squids have high power and mobility and can also have high HP, stealth, and intelligence depending on the build.  
* However, if you want a premier stealth and intelligence build, you're going to want to choose eight arms instead of 10\. This unlocks the octopus class. Octopuses have the second highest intelligence of all marine builds, outplaced only by dolphins. His high intelligence unlocks attributes such as tool use, complex structure navigation, and adaptive problem solving.  
* And while this is all impressive in its own right, where the octopus really shines is using its high intelligence in conjunction with the highest stealth in the entire game. No other build possesses so many strong stealth abilities. Not only can they change the color of their body to match their surroundings, they can also change the texture of their skin so that they're almost indistinguishable from the surface they hide on.  
* In combining these abilities with their high intelligence allows them to shapeshift and mimic other players. And if all of this fails, they've still got their trademark ink ability, which gives it a massive buff to stealth for a split second, just enough to escape and cloak again. Now, I'm sure by now you're all asking where these guys belong on the tier list.  
* If you've watched my earlier videos, you might have seen that I put them in a tier, in which case you're probably wondering how builds with such good stats and abilities can be anywhere but S tier. The problem is they've got some serious weaknesses that can keep them down. First, and most obvious is that they have one of the lowest defense ratings of any marine build.  
* Most mollisks have armor, but in order to maximize stealth and mobility, seephalopods have none. This means that getting caught can mean game over instantly for even the best octopus players. The second less obvious one is that unfortunately for these builds, the total game time is relatively short, capping out at only a few years.  
* This means that sephalopods don't get as much out of their high intelligence as they could. They can solve problems in the moment, but intelligence is most powerful when used to accumulate wisdom. High wisdom accumulation is what makes high intelligence builds truly overpowered. It's what gives elephants their unparalleled pathf finding abilities.  
* It's what gives crows their unmatched strategic prowess and what gives humans the ability to fine-tune their tool use. So, there you have it. An extremely viable build with a multitude of high and even maxed stats held back by only a few weaknesses. If you've got an aspect of the meta you'd like me to talk about, don't hesitate to comment.  
* I've seen some awesome ideas in the comments already that I can't wait to talk about. If you're really desperate to know what I think of your favorite build, you can also subscribe to my Patreon. So, if you aren't interested in hearing me talk about my channel's progress so far, this is your chance to click away. Anyways, guys, this channel is doing ridiculously well.  
* In all of my experience with YouTube, the only way I've ever hit high view counts is through Reddit. However, my last video, the one about snakes, didn't really get much traction on Reddit. But in spite of that, it still did well on YouTube. That's actually huge. It means my videos are being promoted in the suggested videos on YouTube itself.  
* That means the channel will continue to grow. and won't have to rely on lucky breaks on social media. There are very few channels which have entered this phase of growth as quickly as this channel has. In addition, even though my first video has about five times as many views as my others, my new ones receive much more of what YouTube calls viewer engagement.  
* Things like comments, shares, and likes. This means one thing. My subscribers are awesome. You guys got me this far, and I fully intend on making the investments you've made on this channel worth it. Finally, I need to do a shout out to the guys from Two Best Friends Play. I had no idea who they were before starting this channel, but they mentioned me in a podcast which ended up generating a ton of interest for me.  
* I don't deserve the love I've gotten from you guys, as well as those of you who keep posting my videos on the two best friends subreddit. Actually, the same goes for anyone who shares my videos on any subreddit. If I see a video of mine getting a lot of up votes, I'll give you Reddit gold as a way of saying thank you.  
* Anyways, thanks again everyone.
